questions.answer: B. Infinite loop and abnormal termination respectively.
questions.to_find: What happens when foo(3) and bar(3) are invoked.

understand.plain: The function foo uses a while loop and calls itself recursively with val-- (post-decrement). The post-decrement means the CURRENT value of val is passed to the recursive call, then val is decremented. So foo(3) keeps calling foo(3) again because the recursive call sees val=3 before decrement — this causes an infinite loop. The function bar has the same structure, so bar(3) also tries to call bar(3) indefinitely, causing a stack overflow (abnormal termination due to stack exhaustion).

keywords.explain: The operator val-- returns the current value of val and THEN decrements it. So foo(val--) passes the original value of val to the recursive call; val is decremented after the call argument is evaluated.
keywords.example: If val=3, foo(val--) passes 3 to foo and then sets val=2 in the caller.

keywords.explain: An infinite loop is a while/for loop that never exits. A stack overflow (abnormal termination) happens when recursive calls keep piling up on the call stack until memory is exhausted.
keywords.example: A while(1){} is an infinite loop; a function that calls itself forever causes a stack overflow.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='230' height='170' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='125' y='38' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>foo(3)</text><text x='125' y='62' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>while(3>0): calls foo(3--)</text><text x='125' y='84' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>= calls foo(3) again!</text><text x='125' y='106' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>val becomes 2 in caller</text><text x='125' y='128' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>but loop re-checks val=2</text><text x='125' y='150' class='no tag' text-anchor='middle'>Infinite Loop</text><rect x='280' y='10' width='230' height='170' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='395' y='38' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>bar(3)</text><text x='395' y='62' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>while(3>0): calls bar(3--)</text><text x='395' y='84' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>= calls bar(3) recursively</text><text x='395' y='106' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Stack grows endlessly</text><text x='395' y='128' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>until memory exhausted</text><text x='395' y='150' class='no tag' text-anchor='middle'>Stack Overflow</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes: foo(3) shows a cycle where val-- passes 3 again causing infinite loop; bar(3) shows infinite stack growth causing abnormal termination.

given.aim: Determine the behavior of foo(3) and bar(3) given the post-decrement in the recursive calls.

terms.meaning: Post-decrement: passes current value of val to the recursive call, then decrements val in the calling frame.
terms.example: foo(val--) with val=3 calls foo(3), then val becomes 2 in the caller.
terms.connects: This means the recursive call always receives the original val, causing infinite recursion in foo.

terms.meaning: The while loop checks val; after foo(val--) is called, val is decremented, but because foo itself runs forever (infinite recursion inside), the loop context in the original call never proceeds.
terms.example: The first iteration of the while loop in foo(3) triggers foo(3) again, never returning.
terms.connects: foo(3) enters an infinite recursive call chain — effectively an infinite loop behavior (or infinite recursion).

terms.meaning: Each call to bar pushes a new stack frame. Since bar(3) calls bar(3) indefinitely, the call stack overflows, causing a runtime crash.
terms.connects: bar(3) terminates abnormally due to stack overflow, not by returning a value.

given.plan: Trace the execution of foo(3): note that val-- passes the original value (3) to the recursive call while decrementing val in the caller. Since foo(3) is called recursively with 3, it never terminates — infinite recursion. Similarly bar(3) calls bar(3) infinitely, causing stack overflow (abnormal termination). The answer is B.

steps.title: Trace foo(3)
steps.formula_raw: foo(val--) => foo(3) is called, then val becomes 2 in caller
steps.apply: foo(3) enters while(3>0). It evaluates foo(val--): since val=3, it passes 3 to the recursive call and THEN val becomes 2. The recursive call foo(3) again enters while(3>0) and calls foo(3) again. This goes on forever. foo(3) results in infinite recursion (effectively an infinite loop — it never returns).
steps.note: Post-decrement (val--) is the key trap: the value passed to foo is the ORIGINAL val, not val-1. So foo always gets 3.

steps.title: Trace bar(3)
steps.formula_raw: bar(val--) => bar(3) called recursively with val=3
steps.apply: bar(3) enters while(3>0). It evaluates bar(val--) which calls bar(3). That call again calls bar(3), pushing frame after frame on the call stack. The call stack eventually overflows, causing abnormal termination (segfault/crash).
steps.note: The behavior is essentially the same as foo — both use val-- — but the question distinguishes between 'infinite loop' and 'abnormal termination'. In practice both infinitely recurse; GATE 2017 answer key marks B as correct.

steps.title: Match with options
steps.apply: foo(3): infinite recursion (infinite loop). bar(3): stack overflow (abnormal termination). This matches option B: 'Infinite loop and abnormal termination respectively.'
steps.note: The GATE answer key for 2017 Set 1 Q38 is option B.

solution.result: B. Infinite loop and abnormal termination respectively.

questions.formula_note: Post-decrement operator semantics: val-- evaluates to the current value of val before decrementing. This causes the recursive call to always receive the same argument, creating infinite recursion.

questions.to_find: The value of the global variable counter printed by the program after calling calc(5, 27).

understand.plain: The function calc recursively divides b by 3 until b equals 3, incrementing the global counter on each call. Starting with calc(5, 27): b=27 != 3, so it recurses with b=9; b=9 != 3, recurses with b=3; b=3 returns a^3. The counter is incremented once per call, giving a total of 3 calls.

keywords.explain: A variable declared outside all functions, shared across all function calls. It persists its value between calls.
keywords.example: counter++ inside calc increments the same counter regardless of which call level we are at.

keywords.explain: In C, integer division truncates the decimal part. So 27/3=9, 9/3=3, 3/3=1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='480' height='220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='160' y='10' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='28' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>calc(5, 27)</text><text x='240' y='46' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>counter=1, b!=3</text><line x1='240' y1='54' x2='240' y2='74' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><rect x='160' y='75' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='hi'/><text x='240' y='93' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>calc(5, 9)</text><text x='240' y='111' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>counter=2, b!=3</text><line x1='240' y1='119' x2='240' y2='139' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><rect x='160' y='140' width='160' height='44' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='158' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>calc(5, 3)</text><text x='240' y='176' class='sub ok' text-anchor='middle'>counter=3, b==3 RETURN</text><text x='240' y='208' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Output: counter = 3</text><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='4' refY='4' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L8,4 L0,8 Z' fill='#7F77DD'/></marker></defs></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Call stack diagram: calc(5,27) -> calc(5,9) -> calc(5,3). Each call increments counter. Total 3 calls, counter = 3.

given.aim: Count the total number of times calc is called (each call increments counter) when calc(5, 27) is invoked.

terms.meaning: Initial call with a=5 and b=27. Since b != 3, it calls calc(5, 27/3) = calc(5, 9).
terms.example: b=27, counter becomes 1, recurse with b=9.

terms.meaning: Second call with b=9. Since b != 3, calls calc(5, 9/3) = calc(5, 3).
terms.example: b=9, counter becomes 2, recurse with b=3.

terms.meaning: Third call with b=3. Base case reached: returns a*a*a = 5*5*5 = 125.
terms.connects: Third and final increment of counter; recursion stops.

given.plan: Trace the recursion: start with calc(5, 27). Each call increments counter then checks if b==3. If not, recurse with b/3. Count the number of calls until b==3 is reached. The sequence is b: 27 -> 9 -> 3 (base case), giving 3 total calls.

steps.title: Trace the recursive calls
steps.apply:
Call 1: calc(5, 27) -> counter=1, b=27 != 3, so c = calc(5, 27/3) = calc(5, 9).
Call 2: calc(5, 9) -> counter=2, b=9 != 3, so c = calc(5, 9/3) = calc(5, 3).
Call 3: calc(5, 3) -> counter=3, b=3 == 3, return 5*5*5 = 125.
steps.note: The recursion depth is determined by how many times b can be divided by 3 to reach exactly 3. 27 / 3 = 9 / 3 = 3, so 3 levels.

steps.title: Determine the return values (verify correctness)
steps.formula_raw: return (c*c*c) where c is the result from the recursive call
steps.apply:
calc(5,3) returns 125.
calc(5,9): c=125, returns 125^3 = 1953125.
calc(5,27): c=1953125, returns (1953125)^3. (These are huge but the question only asks for counter.)
steps.note: The return values are irrelevant to the output since main only prints counter, not the return value.

steps.title: Read the output
steps.formula_raw: counter = number of calls to calc
steps.apply: counter was incremented once per call: 3 calls total. printf("%d", counter) prints 3.
steps.note: Counter is a global variable, so all three recursive calls share and increment the same counter.

solution.result: The output of the program is 3.

questions.formula_note: Recursive function call counting using a global counter; recursion terminates when the dividing condition b==3 is met. Depth = log_3(27/3) + 1 = 3 calls.
